titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Charles Hanbury-Williams Papers
titlestmt.author: by Sandra Markham

controlnote.p: This collection contains 5 published digital objects. And those objects are comprised of 573 digital files.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday December 5, 2024 at 21:01

did.abstract: The Charles Hanbury-Williams Papers primarily contain correspondence and other materials that document his diplomatic activities in the political, social, and cultural life at the royal courts in Berlin, Dresden, Saint Petersburg, and Warsaw in the 1740s and 1750s, as well as those of his predecessor envoys George Woodward (1697-1735) in Warsaw and Melchior Guy Dickens (1695-1775) in Saint Petersburg. Hanbury-Williams's domestic, personal, literary, and family papers are present in smaller quantity, but those files encompass his friends and family in London and Monmouthshire, Wales, and his parliamentary constituents and friends in Herefordshire, England. Significantly represented in the files are Hanbury-Williams's colleagues Henry Harris, Thomas Winnington, and Henry Fox, Baron Holland; his Hanbury brothers; his wife Lady Frances Coningsby Hanbury-Williams and her father Lord Thomas Coningsby; and his two daughters Lady Frances ("Fanny") Hanbury-Williams Essex and Charlotte Hanbury-Williams Walsingham.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Formerly in the collection of Sir Thomas Phillipps of Cheltenham (MS 10876-10944 and MS 11374-11410). Purchased from William H. Robinson Ltd., June 1949. Bequest of Wilmarth Sheldon Lewis (Yale 1918), 1979. Phillipps MS 10886 purchased from H. P. Kraus, 1981.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: I. Diplomatic Papers, 1720-1758. II. Domestic and Political Papers, 1734-1759. III. Personal Papers, 1717-1774. IV. Capell, Coningsby, and Hanbury Family Papers, 1584-1808. V. Binding Remnants, undated.

bioghist.head: Charles Hanbury-Williams, 1708-1759
bioghist.p: Sir Charles Hanbury-Williams (1708-1759) was a Welsh politician, diplomat, and poet. He was born in London, the fourth of seven sons born to John Hanbury (1664-1734) of Pontypool, Wales, and his second wife Bridget Ayscough (-1741). The Hanbury family made their fortune from Welsh iron mines. In 1729 Charles Hanbury assumed a second surname of Williams, from Charles Williams (1634-1720) who had left a fortune to John Hanbury which was passed on to his son Charles. After attending Eton College and taking a grand tour, Hanbury-Williams returned to London and in 1732 married Lady Frances Coningsby (1708-1781), a daughter of Lord Thomas Coningsby (1657-1729) of Hampton Court in Herefordshire. Upon Coningsby's death she inherited half of his estate, and the remainder in 1761 when her older and only sibling Margaret Coningsby Newton died. Hanbury-Williams and his wife had two children: Frances ("Fanny") (1735-1759), who married William Anne Holles Capel (1732-1799), 4th Earl Essex; and Charlotte (1738-1790), who married Robert Boyle Walsingham (1736-1780). The Hanbury-Williams marriage foundered on the issues of his infidelity and was ended by September 1742 over the advanced case of syphilis he had given his wife.

Hanbury-Williams held a number of posts during his lifetime, both by election and appointment. He was elected to Parliament for Monmouthshire (1735-1747), and 1737 was appointed Paymaster of Marines (resigned 1746). In 1741 he was appointed Lord Lieutenant of Herefordshire (resigned 1745), and in 1754 again elected to Parliament, but for Leominster in Herefordshire. He became a Knight of the Bath in 1744. A popular society figure in London, Hanbury-Williams joined the Society of Dilettanti in 1736 and was elected to White's Club in 1743.

Most remembered for his diplomatic appointments, Hanbury-Williams served as minister to the Electorate of Saxony at Dresden (1747-1750 and 1754-1755) and the Kingdom of Prussia at Berlin (1750-1753). In 1755 he took his final post as envoy to the Russian Empire at Saint Petersburg, before returning to England in 1758. He spent his last two years plagued and incapacitated by physical and mental illnesses, likely the effects of tertiary syphilis, and died in London on November 2, 1759. Charles Hanbury-Williams's remains were interred in the north choir aisle of Westminster Abbey near those of his patron Charles Williams; there is no monument for him.

A concise biography of Hanbury-Williams by Mary Margaret Stewart (2008) appears in the Dictionary of National Biography ; the standard biography has been The Life of Sir Charles Hanbury-Williams, Poet, Wit and Diplomatist , by the Earl of Ilchester and Mrs. Langford-Brooke (London: T. Butterworth Ltd., 1928). A resource for Hanbury family genealogy is Richard Hanbury Tenison's The Hanburys of Monmouthshire (1995).

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Tone Sundt Urstad, in her unpublished 1987 University of Cambridge dissertation on Hanbury-Williams, describes the library's Hanbury-Williams collection as comprising between six and seven thousand items, and relates their provenance as originating from "two different sources, the major part from the Hanbury Williams family at Coldbrook [in Wales], and about four hundred letters from Lord Essex at Cassiobury [Watford, England]." The majority of the papers descended to Hanbury-Williams's great-nephew Ferdinand Hanbury-Williams (1799-1887) when he inherited Coldbrook; he sold them in 1841 to Sir Thomas Phillipps (1792-1872), a collector of books and manuscripts.

Separately, George Capel-Coningsby, 5th Earl of Essex (1757–1839) had given the family letters to the firm of Jeffery and King to have them bound, and then neglected to retrieve them. The firm went into bankruptcy and sold the letters to the bookseller James Bohn, who sold them to Phillipps in 1840.

The items in the library's Charles Hanbury-Williams Papers appear in Thomas Phillipps's Catalogus librorum manuscriptorum in bibliotheca ([Middle Hill]: Impressus typis Medio-Montanis mense maio, 1837 [-1871]), in two blocks: the "Sir Charles Hanbury Williams' MSS" on pages 179-180, bearing Phillipps numbers 10876 through 10944 (minus "bundles" 10921-10923 and "packets" 10941-10943) and the "Supplement to Sir Ch. H. William's MSS" on pages 193-194 and 201, bearing Phillipps numbers 11374 through 11410 and 11660. The latter block cites Hanbury-Williams's own volume numbers in their entries, which the first block does not. Also present in the library's Hanbury-Williams Papers are Phillipps volumes 23965 and 26018, which do not appear in his printed catalogue.

Thomas Phillipps's collection was dispersed in a series of sales presided over by his grandson Thomas Fitzroy Fenwick (1856-1938). When he died, the residue passed to his nephew Alan George Fenwick who, in 1945, sold the remaining material to the London antiquarian booksellers Lionel and Philip Robinson. Wilmarth S. Lewis purchased the papers described in this collection guide from William H. Robinson Ltd. in June 1949. One additional volume (Hanbury-Williams number 26, Phillipps number 10886, in Box 51) was purchased from the New York dealer H. P. Kraus in 1981.

With the 1949 purchase came an inventory of the Hanbury-Williams papers made by Thomas FitzRoy Phillipps Fenwick; this manuscript has been cataloged separately as LWL MSS 7a and is not included in this collection guide.

For further information, see "The Works of Sir Charles Hanbury-Williams" by Tone Sundt Urstad, in the Lewis Walpole Library, and The Life of Sir Charles Hanbury-Williams , by the Earl of Ilchester and Mrs. Langford-Brooke (London: Thornton Butterworth Ltd, 1928).

otherfindaid.head: Hanbury-Williams Card Indexes
otherfindaid.p: An additional way of accessing content in the Charles Hanbury-Williams Papers are the three sets of paper card and slip files in the library's reading room. These files were created in the 1950s by the editors of the Yale Edition of Horace Walpole's Correspondence (working in room 331B in Yale's Sterling Memorial Library), once Lewis had the collection sent to campus after it arrived in Connecticut in 1949. The editorial team read every letter and document present—including those in foreign languages—and indexed each item by date, correspondent, and subject. The three groupings are: 1) six drawers of chronological cards filed by undated material, then 1584-1808. with each card giving a precis of the content of the item; 2) two drawers of typed paper slips with names of correspondents (both author and recipient); and 3) four drawers of typed "subject" slips that index the names of people mentioned within letters and documents and give the numbers of the Hanbury-Williams (two digits) and Phillipps (five digits) volume and the page ("fol.") number in that volume. Those cards are for names only; no topics, events, or places were indexed. None of the card or slip files described above reveal biographical clues to occupations, relationships, or other personal or political contexts for the people mentioned; they are simply names with volume and page numbers.

However, as an example of their value, the folder for Friedrich Benedict Oertel (Box 19, folder 34) holds eight letters the Prussian magistrate sent to Hanbury-Williams in the months prior to his 1748 death, and three that his family members sent soon afterward. The correspondent slips record all eleven letters, but the subject slips reveal that Oertel was mentioned in nine other letters Hanbury-Williams received from his diplomatic correspondents Arnett, Fritsch, Gervinus, and Perrone di San Martino, as well as in three letters from the British Grand Tour tutor Walter Harte.

When using the correspondent and subject indexes, it is important to note that the Walpole editors at that time performed no authority or standardization work on names, which were transcribed just as they appear in a signature or within a letter or document; as a consequence, multiple slips were created for some individuals reflecting each of the variant spellings of their names. Equally important, many of those names will not match with those in this collection guide, which employed the Library of Congress's Name Authority File and other standard sources for constructing correspondents' names (see the Processing Information section of this guide). For instance, letters from Lord and Viscount Stormont are filed under Mansfield, David Murray, Earl of, and those from Hanbury-Williams's secretary Stanislas Poniatowski are under his authorized name of Stanis?aw II August, King of Poland.

To locate a letter or document found in one of the library's card sets, where the card will show a citation such as "50-10918 fol. 22-23" (which is Hanbury-Williams volume 50-10918 Phillipps volume, pages 22-23) keyword search the PDF version of this collection guide for the Phillipps volume number. If the item remains in a bound volume, request that box. If not, search for the person's name to find an entry with dates that match or encompass the item sought, and request that folder and box.

These informative and extensive sets of approximately 14,000 cards and slips do not provide a complete index to the papers but, nevertheless, they are a useful resource—the "subject" slips in particular. Again, these paper-based files can only be accessed in the library's reading room. They are not digitized and the information on them was not incorporated in this collection guide.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Charles Hanbury-Williams Papers. The Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Upon arrival in Wilmarth Lewis's collection, the ninety-four volumes of papers were shelved by their Hanbury-Williams volume numbers, with library staff creating access inventories by both Hanbury-Williams and Phillipps volume numbers. The volumes ranged in size from octavo to folio. Most of the diplomatic material was either created in bound volumes (such as the chronological copies of his outgoing letters) or were bound later by theme (all incoming letters from a secretary of state or a cohort of British envoys). In general, the domestic and family correspondence appears to have been gathered randomly and bound somewhat by sheet size or approximate date. Many of the volumes held papers that extended beyond their covers, so that material was being damaged both in use and storage. In the late 1990s the library decided to disbind nearly half of the collection—forty-three of the ninety-four volumes—and to house some of the loose papers in archival folders and boxes and some laying loose within their covers in phase boxes. The Hanbury-Williams volume number and page or leaf ("fol.") number was written on each piece of loose paper.

Though a rearrangement of the papers was proposed in 1997, the papers remained in their mixed-housing format in order by Hanbury-Williams volume and page numbers until 2023, when the collection was reorganized into an intellectual arrangement of four distinct series that provide a clearer understanding of Hanbury-Williams's diplomatic career and, as important, that separate and highlight his domestic relationships and activities in England and Wales. As an example, the correspondence between Hanbury-Williams and his daughter Charlotte formerly appeared throughout seventeen different albums (his volumes number 31, 52, 54, 58-61, 66-67, 71-72, 75-79, and 82-83) and it is now held in four folders in Box 44.

For material that remains bound together, the Hanbury-Williams and Phillipps numbers are given in each volume's headnote so if that level of access is required, this guide can be keyword searched to locate a particular volume. The empty cases (covers and spines) from disbound volumes form Series V in the papers, and a list of the volumes with both Hanbury-Williams and Phillipps numbers can be seen in that section of this guide. Many of the volumes also have a third number that had been written in ink on the front cover label; it is not known who assigned those numbers or when, but they are also mentioned in the headnote as tertiary numbers.

Another decision made in 2023 was to identify and validate both foreign and domestic names in the papers. The author signatures and names docketed by Hanbury-Williams or his secretary were checked first in the Library of Congress's Name Authority File, and if not found there, in sources such as the Dictionary of National Biography . Refinements and several new identifications were possible as so many online sources—including digitized books and newspapers, and databases such as Biografisch Portaal van Nederland (http://www.biografischportaal.nl/), Deutsche Biographie (https://www.deutsche-biographie.de/home), and Die Sächsische Biografie (https://saebi.isgv.de/)—were not available to the Walpole editors in the 1950s. When possible, names were upgraded to Library of Congress standards, and others were newly identified: Russian diplomat "Count Bestoucheff" has an authorized name of Aleksei? Petrovich Bestuzhev-Ri?u?min, and "Baron Hoensleben" was found to be the Hanoverian envoy Johann Friedrich Karl Alvensleben. Some people, such as D. H. B. Swansbel (per Walpole editors, and alternately spelled Schwanbel by Hanbury-Williams), were not conclusively identified.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: The Reference Library at the Newport Central Library on John Frost Square in Newport, Wales, holds twenty-five volumes of Sir Charles Hanbury-Williams's papers. Among the records are volumes of dispatches from successive secretaries of state (1752–1757), two volumes of letters and instructions from the secretary of state (September 1754 to March 1755), a volume of correspondence (1756–1757) between Andrew Mitchell in Berlin and Hanbury-Williams, a diary kept by Hanbury-Williams during his residence at Berlin (July 1750 to March 1751), and some of Hanbury-Williams's writings on foreign affairs. Also there are volumes of correspondence between the secretary of state and Thomas Robinson, the British envoy at Dresden (1732-1734), some literary papers, and loose correspondence with Robert Walpole, Henry Pelham, Henry Fox, Lord Chesterfield, Horace Walpole, and various ministers and friends of Hanbury Williams. The materials were presented to the Newport Central Library by Ruth Hanbury Tenison, daughter of John Capel Hanbury of Pontypool Park, on April 15, 1924.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Thomas FitzRoy Phillipps Fenwick, Sir Charles Hanbury Williams MSS Diplomatic & Letters and Papers Together with Private Letters (LWL MSS 7a). Lewis Walpole Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Charles Hanbury-Williams Papers contains correspondence and other materials that document his activities in the political, social, and cultural life at the royal courts in Berlin, Dresden, Saint Petersburg, and Warsaw in the 1740s and 1750s, as well as those of his predecessor envoys George Woodward (1697-1735) and Melchior Guy Dickens (1695-1775). Hanbury-Williams's political, personal, literary, and family papers are present in smaller quantity, but those files encompass his friends and family in London and in Monmouthshire, Wales, and his parliamentary constituents and friends in Herefordshire, England. Significantly represented in the correspondence files are Hanbury-Williams's colleagues Henry Harris, Thomas Winnington, and Henry Fox, Baron Holland; his Hanbury brothers; his wife Lady Frances Coningsby Hanbury-Williams and her father Lord Thomas Coningsby; and his two daughters Frances ("Fanny") Hanbury-Williams Capel, Lady Essex, and Charlotte Hanbury-Williams Walsingham.

The eight linear feet of material in Series I, Diplomatic Papers, comprises the greatest part of the collection; only eight of the thirty-five boxes in the series contain material that was removed from bound volumes, so this series is the most intact from the arrangement created by Hanbury-Williams himself. Series II, Domestic and Political Papers, is two feet of correspondence with his constituents, friends, and colleagues in Southern Wales, Herefordshire, Bath, and London; all the material here was removed from bound volumes and filed in boxes and folders for easier access to letters from specific people. Series III, Personal Papers, also measures two linear feet and holds important correspondence between Hanbury-Williams, his wife, and two daughters, as well as with his brothers, and between his daughters and their uncles; these letters are personal and reveal the landscape of Hanbury-Williams's life and the tragedy of his later years. Also here are literary works, primarily poems, by Hanbury-Williams and others. A few estate papers close out the series. Series IV., measuring under two linear feet, Is the smallest part of the papers, and holds third-party correspondence between family members and their friends and colleagues. Series V. contains only the empty cases from the volumes of correspondence that has been disbound.

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: Four letters from Horace Walpole—three to Charles Hanbury-Williams and one to his daughter Frances ("Fanny") Hanbury-Williams Capel, Lady Essex—were removed from this collection and filed into the library's Horace Walpole Collection (LWL MSS 1). The letters to Hanbury-Williams were formerly in his volume 52 (Phillipps 10902), and to Lady Essex in volume 64 (Phillipps 10911). They can be located in the LWL MSS 1 finding guide as well as in Archives at Yale and in the print and online versions of Yale Edition of Horace Walpole's Correspondence .

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Charles Hanbury-Williams Papers is the physical property of the Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the W. S. Lewis Librarian/Executive Director.

Essex, William Anne Holles Capel, Earl of, 1732-1799
Harrington, William Stanhope, Earl of, approximately 1690-1756
Huybert, Petrus Anthonius de, Heer Van Kruiningen, 1693-1763
Marteville, Louis Chrétien de Pierre de, 1706-1760
Rochford, William Henry Nassau de Zuylestein, Earl of, 1717-1781
Stanisław, King of Poland, II August, 1732-1798

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains the bulk of Charles Hanbury-Williams's papers, and the material not only spans his entire diplomatic career, but takes in papers of two of the ministers that held posts in Warsaw and Saint Petersburg before him. Hanbury-Williams received his first appointment as minister to the Electorate of Saxony in December 1746, and for a decade beginning in April 1747, he moved through a series of appointments in Dresden, Berlin, Warsaw, and Saint Petersburg at the courts of Saxony, Prussia, Poland, and Russia, before returning to England in February 1758. For that reason, his cohort of correspondents—British and foreign—remained fairly constant even though his location would change.

Much of the material in Series I is held in bound volumes. The Correspondence subseries contains both incoming and outgoing (in fair copy) letters discussing a wide variety of diplomatic negotiations, as well as reports on local events interspersed with intelligence and court gossip. Also here is Hanbury-Williams's correspondence with the vendors and bankers that moved merchandise meant for him and to others as court gifts. The Documents subseries contains correspondence, pro memoria, copies of treaties, and various historical documents meant to inform Hanbury-Williams about past negotiations. Also present here is a volume related to the disposition of the Ostrog region which was was of great interest to the Poniatowski family, and Hanbury-Williams's support lead to a fracture in his relationship with Count Brühl. The subseries Papers of Prior Envoys holds bound volumes of diplomatic records left by the British ministers George Woodward and Melchior Guy Dickens who preceded Hanbury-Williams in Dresden, Warsaw, and Saint Petersburg.

The contents of the bound volumes are described overall in headnotes; in many cases the items enclosed have been either listed by page number or indexed by author or recipient. His outgoing correspondence was generally recorded in chronological order but the incoming letters were grouped in various ways before being bound. For ease of access to letters from particular envoys, their names were listed in alphabetical order and the relevant page numbers in the volume were cited. Titles of diplomatic docouments were transcribed as they were written; abbreviations and spellings were preserved and diacritics were not added.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into three subseries: Correspondence, Documents, and Papers of Prior Envoys.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under three headings: Secretary of State and Staff; British Envoys, Politicians, and Others; and Foreign Correspondents.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged under two headings: Incoming Letters, and Outgoing Letters.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 266 pages of letters primarily from Philip Dormer Stanhope, Earl of Chesterfield, and Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle, writing from Whitehall in London. Also present are letters of instruction from George II appointing Hanbury-Williams Envoy Extraordinary at the Court of the King of Poland in 1747 (pages 13-24), his letter of revocation in 1749 for reassignment to the Court of the King of Prussia (pages 199-202), and his instructions from King George II for travel to Anspach to invest Charles William Frederick, the Margrave of Anspach, with the Ensigns of the Order of the Garter (pages 263-265). The letters in the volume were bound nearly in chronological order.

Other items in the volume are a copy of a letter written in 1715 to George Townshend from members of the Board of Trade (pages 1-8) and a copy of Lord Harrington's letter to all ministers abroad regarding court couriers, with a list of charges for their trips between Whitehall or Hanover and foreign cities (pages 9-12). Near the end of the volume (pages 243-262), is a "Paper delivered by Count Fleming," in which Saxon minister Karl Georg Friedrich Flemming mentions the June 1747 "double wedding" of Bavarian Elector Maximilian Joseph and his sister Princess Maria Antonia to the Electoral Prince Friedrich Christian of Saxony and his sister Princess Maria Anna; the marriage united the ruling families of Bavaria and Saxony.

The volume is untitled; it is in a stiff-board binding covered in brown paper with a blue linen spine and has no label on the front cover. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 34; the Phillipps number is 10906.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 480 pages of letters from Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle, bound in chronological order. At the front of the book are two letters of instruction from King George II appointing Hanbury-Williams as Envoy Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary to the court of the King of Prussia in Berlin; a third letter of instruction for his appointment to the Court of the King of Poland (January 22, 1751) appears at page 203.

"Instructions and Letters From the Secretary of State Received at Berlin and Dresden" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 13; the Phillipps number is 10917, with a tertiary number of 43.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 368 pages of copies of letters from Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle and Secretary of State, to Hanbury-Williams; the material is in English, Latin, and French, and replicates some of the material that appears in volume 13. Volume 16 opens with letters from King George II and from Newcastle to the King and Queen of Prussia introducing Hanbury-Williams, and instructions from the King to Hanbury-Williams, appointing him as Envoy Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary to the Court of Prussia in June 1750. Copies of letters from Newcastle to Hanbury-Williams begin on page 19. In addition to transcriptions of those letters, there are also letters Newcastle sent to other British foreign ministers, as well as pro memoria and various diplomatic documents. The volume ends with Hanbury-Williams's instructions from the King appointing him to the Court of the King of Poland in Warsaw, dated June 20, 1754.

"Papers Relating to my Legation at Berlin & Dresden Being Letters from the Sec. of State to S.C.H.W. from 1750 to 1753" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with an octagonal paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the red leather spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 16; the Phillipps number is 10897, with a tertiary number of 23.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 128 pages of copies of letters from Robert Darcy, Earl of Holderness, to Hanbury-Williams and Robert Keith, along with copies of documents relating to "the Nature of the Disputes which have arisen between His Majesty & The Court of France, in America." Some of the material concerns negotiations with the Duc de Mirepoix and Thomas Robinson. It is in English and French, and dates between January and April 1755, with some papers referencing earlier events.

"America" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a small octagonal paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 24; the Phillipps number is 10919, with a tertiary number of 45.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 356 pages of fair copies of Hanbury-Williams's letters to the secretaries of state for the Northern Division, Philip Stanhope, Earl of Chesterfield (June 2, 1747, through mid-March 1748) and Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle (thereafter) sent from his post in Dresden and while on a visit to Leipzig (May 1749); some letters are addressed to under-secretaries William Chetwynd and Andrew Stone.

The first items in the book are copies of letters from King George II to the King and Queen of Poland in February 1747, and a copy of a post from the East India Company about the plunder of Fort St. George in Madras; letters to the secretaries begin on page 5. Also present are letters to Thomas Robinson, Earl of Grantham and ambassador to Vienna (June 9 through July 9, 1747) and to John Carmichael, Earl of Hyndford and ambassador to Russia (October 1748). The last letter in the volume, written to Newcastle on August 13, 1749, fills eight pages and was sent as Hanbury-Williams's final post from Dresden.

"Correspondence with His Majestys Ministers at Foreign Courts" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover, and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 10; the Phillipps number is 10882, with a tertiary number of 7.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 692 pages of letters and reports sent primarily to Secretary of State Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle, with reports, documents, and copies of letters sent as enclosures between Hanbury-Williams, Newcastle, and other diplomats. Hanbury-Williams titled this volume "Papers of Consequence," but he did not otherwise impose a discernable order to the materials, in both French and English, that he had accumulated between 1748 and 1751, as he moved between Dresden, Berlin, and Dresden again.

The volume opens with a significant set of diplomatic reports meant for the King's eyes. The other reports vary in topic and substance with some just recording conversations or presentations. Among the reports is one titled "Portrait du Roi de P.," in which the author, presumably Hanbury-Williams, states that the King of Poland has the most beautiful eyes in the world.

"Papers of Consequence" is in a stiff-board binding covered in light blue paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover, and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 29; the Phillipps number is 10924, with a tertiary number of 50.

did.unittitle: 1-105: Three reports on Dresden and the state of affairs in electorates

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The first report (56 pages) is Hanbury-Williams's final report on the state of affairs in Dresden, with character studies of members of the Polish court; nine full pages are given to a description of Count Heinrich Brühl, who is described as "the most extraordinary man and Minister that this age or perhaps any other has produc'd." The second (39 pages) and third (10 pages) discuss alliances with electorates and the Kingdom of Poland.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 263 pages of fair copies of Hanbury-Williams's letters to Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle, in chronological order, as sent from Berlin and Warsaw. Among other topics covered are rumors of the location and travels of Charles Edward Stuart ("The Young Pretender") and the establishment of the Emden Company, or the Royal Prussian Asiatic Company in Emden to Canton and China, in 1751; in his letter of October 3, 1750, Hanbury-Williams included papers describing the company's organizational scheme.

"Correspondence with the Secretary of State, Berlin" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 11; the Phillipps number is 10901, with a tertiary number of 27.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 359 pages of fair copies of Hanbury-Williams's letters to Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle, sent from Dresden, Leipzig, and Hubertusburg, the latter for treaty negotiations in October and November 1751. Many of Hanbury-Williams's reports concern treaty negotiations with Poland's minister Heinrich Brühl, Russian ambassador Hermann Carl von Keyserlingk, and the Dutch diplomat Cornelis Calkoen. Beginning on page 316, he wrote out "A Short Account of the present State of Poland" that ends with capsule biographies of "the principal Persons, who have the greatest Influence on that Republik."

"Correspondence with the Secretary of State begun at Dresden" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with an octagonal paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the brown leather spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 17; the Phillipps number is 10896, with a tertiary number of 22.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged under two headings: Incoming Letters, and Outgoing Letters.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 188 pages of letters from British envoys to Hanbury-Williams. The letters were grouped by author before being bound but are otherwise in no order; the pages are numbered.

Three items in the book are not letters to Hanbury-Williams and appear not to be related, except by date, to letters in the volume. On pages 104-105 are two documents dated July 19, 1747, and written in Italian, about the Battle of Assietta in the Piedmont: a report from Fenestrelle on the fighting, and a copy of a letter from Count Giovanni Bricherasio to Count Giovanni Battista Bogino. On pages 136-137 is an unsigned document giving the author's thoughts on Europe. It is dated December 27, 1747, and is inscribed at the top in pencil "Lord Ilchester's handwriting" suggesting the author as Stephen Fox-Strangways, Earl of Ilchester.

"Letters from English Ministers at different foreign Courts" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with an octagonal paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 8; the Phillipps number is 10899, with a tertiary number of 25.

scopecontent.p: The volume holds 314 pages of letters from a dozen British diplomats. The album begins with just two letters from 1752, and then proceeds in chronological order through the spring of 1755; the pages are numbered. Of note is a letter from Ruvigny de Cosne, secretary at the British embassy in Paris, notifying Hanbury-Williams of the death of Lord Albemarle by apoplexy in December 1754.

"Letters from English Ministers at Foreign Courts" is in a stiff-board binding covered in blue paper with an octagonal paper label on the cover. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 28; the Phillipps number is 10927 with a tertiary number of 53.

scopecontent.p: The volume holds 263 pages of letters Hanbury-Williams received from six British foreign ministers leading up to and during his service as the ambassador to Russia (appointed in March 1755, left Saint Petersburg for England in October 1755). The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 21; the Phillipps number is 10878, with a tertiary number of 3.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 351 pages of fair copies of letters Hanbury-Williams wrote from Berlin, Dresden, Leipzig, Hubertsburg, and Warsaw to his peers in other cities (likely the locations posted in the incoming letters above). The envoys receiving the majority of the reports are Melchior Guy Dickens in Saint Petersburg, Robert Keith in Vienna, Onslow Burrish in Bavaria, and the Earl of Albemarle in Paris. The single non-British envoy is Count Karl Georg Friedrich Flemming, the Saxon minister in London; letters to him are in French while the rest are in English. The letters were copied in chronological order and run up to Hanbury-Williams's July 1753 departure for England. The final letter in the volume, to Joseph Yorke, was written from London.

The volume has no cover label but is titled inside "Correspondence with his Majesty's Ministers at Foreign Courts," is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, and has printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the red leather spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 14; the Phillipps number 10883 with a tertiary number (written inside) of 50.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds 600 pages of draft letters to British ministers in foreign courts, written from Dresden, Warsaw, and Saint Petersburg, and entered chronologically. The single non-British envoy is the Saxon ambassador Count Karl Georg Friedrich Flemming. The letters are in English and cypher, with the numerical codes written directly over the words they represent.

"Letters from His Excellency Sr. Charles Hanbury Williams to English Ministers at Foreign Courts" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with an octagonal paper label on the front cover, and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 22; the Phillipps number is 10885, with a tertiary number of 10.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The letters in this section came from various foreign diplomats, government officials, counts, and their wives, as well as authors and other cultural figures. An attempt was made to identify the correspondents; brief biographical statements are included for those who could be found, and locations are listed for the elusive. The material is primarily in French.

The letters cover political, social, and business topics. The greatest number are from three diplomats: Louis de Marteville, the Dutch ambassador in Stockholm; Saxon minister Gerlach Adolph Freiherr von Münchhausen; and Stanisław II August, the King of Poland, during the years he served as a secretary to Hanbury-Williams (under his birth name Stanisław August Poniatowski). Also present are letters from Poniatowski's brother and both of his parents. At the other end of the spectrum, Nicholas Gwynne and Daniel Von Heck both wrote from Leyden regarding the Leyden Post Office's expenses in forwarding Hanbury-Williams's dispatches.

Notable cultural figures include the painter Anton Raphael Mengs and the engraver and art historian Jakob von Staehlin in Saint Petersburg; Staehlin wrote to announce the arrival of French sculptor Nicolas-François Gillet (1709-1791) and painter Louis-Joseph Le Lorrain (1715-1759) at the Imperial Academy. There are letters from two French authors, François-Thomas-Marie de Baculard d'Arnaud and Voltaire, and the Swiss author Jean Baptiste Tollot. Musicians are represented by letters from the Florentine contralto Vittoria Tesi Tramontini writing from Vienna, and the Italian-Austrian soprano Regina Mingotti who was then performing in Madrid, plus one from the Italian contralto Pasqualino Bruscolini performing in Warsaw.

Material filed here came from multiple bound volumes that had been disbound in the mid-1990s, including Hanbury-Williams volumes 9, 30, 31, 32, 49, 50, 51, 54, 55, 62, 63, 64, 74, and 95. The letters were foldered alphabetically by author in 2023, with the unidentified correspondents filed at the end.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under three headings: Diplomats and Colleagues, Unidentified Correspondents, and Business Associates.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This section of correspondence holds letters that are unsigned or have indecipherable signatures, incomplete letters (lacking first or second pages, salutations, or signatures), and copies of and extracts from third-party correspondence that have no clear authorship. Most have no docketing to help with identification and, from reading, the authors could not be identified by the Yale Walpole editors or library staff. The material is arranged in chronological order within the folder, when dates are known.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Filed here are letters from bankers, merchants, and companies doing business with or shipping goods to Hanbury-Williams during his time abroad. They range in quantity from multiple communications from the Scottish merchant and banker Archibald Gibsone in Danzig and Frederic Chrestien Gervinus in Dresden, to a just few receipts from the French wine merchant Victor Cliquot, Dresden confiturier Jean Godefroi Gerlach, goldsmiths Jean Jaques Pallard and George Wickes, and an unidentified Dresden tailor. The Wickes invoice, dated 1747, describes numerous items of engraved silver flatware and serving pieces as well as the "iron bound cases with bags and locks" for shipping to Dresden.

Of interest is John Hanbury, a member of the Right Worshipful Society of Merchant Adventurers in Hamburg, and vice-consul of the English factory there; though likely a relative, it is unclear where he falls on the family tree. Aside from engaging in a decade of forwarding freight for the diplomat, he writes on April 2, 1756, with a letter of introduction to Michael Stewart, British equilibrist who had performed in Hamburg and was on his way to Saint Petersburg and Moscow.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Encloses a letter from Calandrini in Geneva.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Encloses an invoice for plate being shipped to Dresden.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in seven volumes and one box.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds copies of treaties and other documents that cover the period from 1660 to 1750. Bound in at the end are letters to Hanbury-Williams from the diplomat Christoph Friedrich von Seckendorff-Aberdar (1679-1759), minister of Brandenburg-Ansbach.

"Treatys and other Papers of no great Consequence" is in a stiff-board binding covered Prussian blue paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 1; the Phillipps number is 10888, with a tertiary number of 14.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in four sections: "Papers relative to my Legation at Berlin," "Papers received at different Times at Dresden," "Papers relative to the Treaty of Subsidy signed the 13th Septr. 1751," and "Correspondence between the Duke of Newcastle, and Lord Hyndford & Mr. Keith."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds fair copies of correspondence, treaties and other documents that date from 1750 to 1752. The contents were arranged in four sections, each with a descriptive cover sheet, and the volume has a table of contents at the front.

"Papers of Consequence bound up in 1753" is in a stiff-board binding covered in light blue paper, with an octagonal paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 27; the Phillipps number is 10925, with a tertiary number of 51.

"Papers relative to the Treaty of Subsidy signed the 13th Septr. 1751"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The third section of the volume holds 143 pages of copies of documents including declarations, memoranda, letters, and agreements related to the Treaty of Subsidy (alliance) between Great Britain, the Netherlands, and Poland, signed at Dresden on September 13, 1751. Correspondents present are Hanbury-Williams and foreign diplomats Heinrich Brühl, Cornelis Calkoen, and Hermann Carl Keyserlingk.

did.unittitle: "Correspondence between the Duke of Newcastle, and Lord Hyndford & Mr. Keith"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The final of the four sections of the volume begins on page 401 and contains 120 pages of copied letters that had circulated between Thomas Pelham-Holles, the Duke Newcastle and secretary of state for the Northern Department; John Carmichael, Earl of Hyndford, and his colleague Robert Keith, British minister at the Court of Vienna; and William Anne Keppel, Earl of Albemarle, in Paris. The contents largely concern Newcastle's tactical diplomatic plan to have Austrian Archduke Joseph elected King of the Romans. The plan failed at that point, although Joseph became the Holy Roman Emperor in 1765.

did.unittitle: Documents and letters regarding the Barrier Treaty copied at Vienna, 1750-1753

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume contains 146 pages of copies of resolutions, pro memoria, declarations, and letters exchanged between diplomats for Austria, Great Britain, and the States General (now Netherlands) about conditions of the Barrier Treaty regarding trade. The material covers the period from April 1750 through 1753 and is in French and English. It was copied out in chronological order, with a three-page table of contents at the end of the volume. Diplomats involved are Willem Bentinck; Barthold Douma van Burmania; Hendrik Fagel; Wenzel Anton, Prince of Kaunitz-Rietberg; Anton Corfiz Ulfeldt; British Secretary of State Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle; and the British envoys Robert Keith and Joseph Yorke. Marginal notes make reference to additional content in "Vol. 1," but that volume is not present in this collection.

"Lettres et Papiers touchant la Barriere et Papiers touchant la Negociation entre La Cour de Vienne et L'Electeur Palatin. Recu à Vienna, Nr. 2" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a an octagonal cut paper on the cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 15; the Phillipps number is 10881, with a tertiary number of 6.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume contains 232 pages of documents and letters dated between 1752 and 1754 which Hanbury-Williams, from its title, considered important. They are in English and French and are bound in no particular arrangment. Some of the material relates to the Ottoman Republic and Poland, and to the Barrier Treaty and financial dealings with the States General. Bound into the middle of the volume (pages 135-150) is the lone non-diplomatic document: a financial accounting of the land holdings of the Earl of Essex, encompassing his properties in Hertfordshire, Essex, Gloucestershire, Norfolk, Suffolk, and Derbyshire.

"Various Papers of Consequence" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with an octagonal paper label on the front cover listing some of the contents, and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 7; the Phillipps number is 10905, with a tertiary number of 31.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume contains 227 pages of manuscript essays and reports related to the Ostrog Ordination, concerning the division of lands and other assets in the Duchy of Ostrog, formerly in southern Poland but now part of Ukraine. The documents are written in several hands in French, and are unsigned, unattributed, and undated.

For further information on Ostrog, see "The Affair of Ostrog: An Episode in Malto-Polish Relations in the Eighteenth Century," by Roderick Cavaliero, in the University of Malta's Journal of the Faculty of Arts 1, issue 2 (1958): 128-41, and Alain Blondy, "L'ordre de Malte et Malte dans les affaires polonaises et russes au XVIIIe siècle," in Revue des études slaves 66, fascicule 4 (1994): 733-55.

The volume is untitled. It is in a stiff-board binding covered in green paper, with printed CH-W and Phillipps labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 36; the Phillipps number is 11378.

scopecontent.p: The volume holds 120 pages of documents relative to diplomatic relations between England and Russia in the early 1750s, including a pro memoria signed by Bestoucheff (Alekseĭ Petrovich Bestuzhev-Ri︠u︡min) and Michael Woronzow (Mikhail Ilarīonovich, graf Voront︠s︡ov).

The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 19; the Phillipps number is 10915, with a tertiary number of 42.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript volume of twenty-two leaves holding nine questions and answers related to Poland; the text is in French. The first question asks if the courts of Vienna, Saint Petersburg, and Dresden are friendly for the succession of the crown of Poland to the Prince Royal; the eighth question asks what are the measures to be taken to ensure the election of the Prince Royal in case the King of Poland dies. Question nine asks what steps have been taken towards Russia, and what response has been received at Dresden from the court of Saint Petersburg on the matter in question.

On page three is an inscription from the Saxon diplomat Ferdinand Ludwig von Saul auf Scherau to Hanbury-Williams stating that he is "sending back to You the excellent work that You had the goodness to prepare for us."

"Questions & responses" is covered in colored paste paper, with the title inscribed in ink on the front cover. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 23; the Phillipps number is 10900, with a tertiary number of 26.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three German shipping claims documents mentioning merchants Georg Friedrich Schwinck, Samuel Watson and Son, Collins Moir and Company, and Wilberforce and Thornton, among others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Copies of articles and agreements between the Duke of Cumberland and Louis François Armand de Vignerot du Plessis, Duc de Richelieu.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subseries contains the papers of two British diplomats, George Woodward in Dresden and Warsaw, and Melchior Guy Dickens in Saint Petersburg. Both men preceded Charles Hanbury-Williams in those posts.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: George Woodward Papers, and Melchior Guy Dickens Papers.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: George Woodward (1697-1735) was a British diplomat in Poland during the early eighteenth century. He was appointed as Resident at the Court of Augustus II on October 22, 1728, and arrived in Dresden in 1729. Woodward remained at Warsaw or Dresden until 1731, when he returned to England. He was sent to Poland once more in February 1732, as Envoy Extraordinary, and reappointed in February 1733. George Woodward died in Warsaw on November 30, 1735, after a few weeks' illness. He was buried with other family members in All Saints Churchyard at Hillesden, Buckinghamshire.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The George Woodward Papers document England's diplomatic efforts in Warsaw and Dresden a decade before Charles Hanbury-Williams arrived in 1747. The records consist of manuscript copies of treaties, agreements, and pro memoria, plus several hundred letters sent between Woodward and the secretaries of state, their staff, and foreign and British diplomats. The material fills six volumes, two of which contain documents and four contain letters. Further description the contents appears at the volume level.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged under two headings: Correspondence, and Documents.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Correspondence subseries contains both incoming letters and copies of Woodward's outgoing letters. Two volumes hold incoming letters, one has incoming and outgoing, and the fourth has only outgoing. The term "foul draughts" in the volume titles refers to the alterations and corrections seen on the drafts; occasionally, Woodward (or his secretary) has noted a portion of the letter that had been sent out in cypher.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged and bound in four volumes.

did.unittitle: Letters from the Secretary of State

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume contains 558 pages of diplomatic appointment papers and letters from three secretaries of state: Charles Townshend, Lord Townshend; William Stanhope, Earl of Harrington; and Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle. Their letters inform him of the King's movements outside of England, of deaths in the royal family (for advising the King of Poland and for mourning rituals to be observed), and of opinions and further instructions from the King. Many letters have sections written in cypher, and some have enclosures such as copies of letters the King had sent to Poland and speeches he gave in Parliament. In their letters, Townshend and Newcastle also comment on or respond to the dispatches Woodward had sent in to Windsor and Whitehall. The material was bound in a chronological order; the pages are numbered.

"Letters from different Secretaries of State viz Lord Townshend, the Duke of Newcastle & Lord Harrington to Mr. Woodward" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 3; the Phillipps number is 10920, with a tertiary number of 46.

did.unittitle: 9-12: Copy of credential letter from George II
13-16: Copy of letter from Queen Caroline
27-30: Copy of credential letter from George II
287-290: Copy of credential letter from George II
Letters from British and foreign envoys

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume held letters Woodward received from diplomatic colleagues on the Continent and in England, including Carel (Charles) Rumpf, the Dutch minister to Warsaw; Prussian minister Baron de Bülow; James Dayrolles, the crown's resident at The Hague; and Edward Weston, an under-secretary for the Northern Department. The bulk of the letters are from another under-secretary, George Tilson, who wrote from London and Hanover. The letters were disbound in the mid-1990s, and were foldered alphabetically by author in 2023.

"Private & Public Correspondence with Mr. Woodward" was in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 47; the Phillipps number is 10890, with a tertiary number of 16.

did.unittitle: Fleury und Beaumont, Franz Joseph Wicardel, Marquis von, -1735

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume contains both incoming and outgoing correspondence. The first half of the volume holds letters that Woodward received from secretaries of state Charles Townshend and William Stanhope, Lord Harrington, writing from London and Windsor, and from British diplomats stationed on the Continent such as Philip Dormer Stanhope, Earl of Chesterfield, in The Hague, George Forbes in Saint Petersburg, and James Waldegrave in Vienna.

The remainder of the volume is filled with drafts of Woodward's outgoing correspondence sent from Warsaw and Dresden between February 10 and December 31, 1729. The letters were bound chronologically and are primarily addressed to Secretary of State George Townshend and his under-secretary George Tilson, with scattered letters to others including Chesterfield, Newcastle, Finch, Sutton, Stanyan, Stanhope, du Bourgay, and Holzendorf; this section of the volume does not have numbered pages.

"Public Letters to Mr Woodward & Foul Draft Public Letters" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 4; the Phillipps number is 10887, with a tertiary number of 12.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: With enclosures: copies of letters to Townshend from George II and Queen Caroline

did.unittitle: 45-48: Copy of a letter from Queen Caroline to the King of Poland
109-128: Claudius Rondeau, Moscow and Saint Petersburg
449 to end: Copies of letters sent

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume holds drafts of letters and posts that Woodward wrote to his political and diplomatic colleagues from Dresden, Leipzig, Canitz, Warsaw, and Grodno, between January 7, 1730, and July 21, 1731. The letters were bound chronologically and are primarily addressed to Secretary of State George Townshend and his under-secretary George Tilson, with some letters to others including letter writers found in the previous volume, such as Harrington, Chesterfield, Newcastle, Guy Dickens, Finch, du Bourgay, Waldegrave, Hotham, Schaub, and Wich, as well as Stephen Poyntz and Carel (Charles) Rumpf, the Dutch minister to Warsaw. The volume's pages are not numbered and the letters are nearly all in English.

"Foul Draughts of Mr. Woodward's private and publick Letters" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 5; the Phillipps number is 10944, with a tertiary number of 13.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Documents subseries consists of two volumes of diplomatic records. It is unclear who bound the papers and when, as well as who assigned the volumes' titles.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized in two volumes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume contains nearly one hundred documents, memoranda, and letters, primarily in contemporary copies, that cover diplomatic efforts between England and Poland in the 1720s and 1730s. As some of the events and papers pre-date George Woodward's appointment, it is unclear if the copies were made at his request or were waiting for him in Warsaw, but as none are dated after his 1735 death it is likely that Charles Hanbury-Williams kept them together as a record of Woodward's diplomatic period. Many items in the album address issues of the community of Protestant dissidents in Poland, with several references to the riot and other events in Toruń. Grouped at the end of the volume (pages 467 to 600) are twenty-two documents dating 1731-1732 regarding the Compagnie d'Ostende and the seizure of their ships in Bengal by the East India Company. Principals involved are from the East India Company, Secretary of State William Stanhope, Earl of Harrington, and Count Watzdorff, the King of Poland's representative in London, because the Ostend ships had sailed under Poland's colors with Polish passports. Also present are depositions from the ships' crew members.
A photocopy of the volume inventory made by Thomas Phillipps's grandson Thomas Fitzroy Fenwick (1856-1938) is housed with it and provides guidance to the general location of the items inside.

"Various Papers of Mr. Woodwards of no great Consequence" is in a stiff-board binding covered in Prussian blue paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 2; the Phillipps number is 10891, with a tertiary number of 17.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume contains forty-six documents that primarily are contemporary copies of agreements, declarations, memoranda, and letters. Items of note are a January 1725 letter from Lord Townshend to Edward Finch, minister plenipotentiary in Poland prior to Woodward's term, about the events at Toruń (p. 71-78), an undated document with examples of the manner of addressing letters to continental rulers (p. 179-200), and a list of expenses allowed to Finch for travel and for mourning the death of the King (p. 201).
A copy of the volume inventory made by Thomas Phillipps's grandson T. FitzRoy Fenwick (1856-1938) is housed with it and provides guidance to the general location of the items inside.

"Various Papers of Mr. Woodwards of no great Consequence" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper, with a cut paper label on the front cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 6; the Phillipps number is 10880, with a tertiary number of 5.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Colonel Melchior Guy Dickens (1695 -1775) had a career in the Army before he was appointed secretary to the British Legation at Berlin in 1730. He served as the minister to Sweden from 1742 to 1748, and then as Envoy Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary in Saint Petersburg from 1749 to 1751 and 1753 to 1755, after which he retired to England. He died at Wadden Croydon, Surrey, England, in 1775. His wife was Hannah Dickens (1706-1752).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A single volume of letters and documents that Guy Dickens passed to Hanbury-Williams when he was appointed ambassador to Russia in March 1755. It holds incoming correspondence from two secretaries of state and from a colleague in Vienna, and copies of the final set of letters Guy Dickens sent to Holderness from his post in Russia before returning to England.

"Papers received from Col. Guy Dickens at St. Petersbourg" is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper with an octagonal paper label on the front cover, and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 20; the Phillipps number is 10884, with a tertiary number of 9.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters and enclosures on pages 41-105 (enclosures: several numbered documents and extracts of letters Holderness had received from Hanbury-Williams in Warsaw, and from James Porter in Constantinople to Thomas Robinson).

did.unittitle: Keith, Robert, in Vienna, to Guy Dickens

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters on pages 114-214.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series holds personal, political, and professional correspondence with Charles Hanbury-Williams's friends and associates in England and Wales. This non-diplomatic correspondence was sorted into two geographical groups to separate his relationships with his Welsh and Herefordshire political constituents and friends from those of his London-based life, in order to provide a clearer view of each universe. Two smaller sets of records, Business Associates and Paymaster of Marines Records, follow the first two subseries and hold correspondence and papers that document the merchants he dealt with and an important political position he held for a decade.

Some of the letters in Series II could also be from colleagues he interacted with—or who wrote to him—while he served in Europe, such as Elizabeth Bosanquet in Hamburg or the historian/author John Campbell in London, but they are filed here because their authors were not involved in court affairs or negotiations. The correspondence in Series I constitutes Hanbury-Williams's official diplomatic record.
The last subseries, Paymaster of Marines Records, holds correspondence and documents related to a position he held from November 1737 through December 1746.

As Charles Hanbury-Williams's health began to fail in 1758, his steward and secretary James Price began to handle his correspondence and business affairs and did so through his death and afterward. A decision was made in 2023 to interfile letters to and from Price as if they were to and from Hanbury-Williams. None of the matters covered concern Price's personal life or business; the content within the letters is expressly for and about Hanbury-Williams.

The letters in this series came from multiple bound volumes that had been disbound in the mid-1990s, including Hanbury-Williams volumes 9, 30, 31, 32, 49, 50, 51, 54, 55, 62, 63, 64, 74, 75, 83, and 95. In 2023 the material was foldered alphabetically by author, with unidentified correspondents filed at the end.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into four subseries: English Friends and Colleagues, Welsh and Herefordshire Friends and Colleagues, Business Associates, and Paymaster of Marines Records.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subseries holds letters from Hanbury-Williams's contacts ranging from his Parliament peers to friends from London and colleagues touring Europe. Many of the writers ask for favors such as an order for porcelain tableware by Sir Henry Oxenden, or a customs appointment in Bristol by John Giles, or Richard Gaven looking to sell a Raphael painting in Dresden. Others offered news and gossip as did John Calcraft, whose twenty letters carry details of political activities and, on January 3, 1755, word of the recent deaths of Lords Albermarle and Levenson-Gower, plus the non-public details of the death by suicide of Henry Bromley, Baron Montfort. John Poulett, Lord of the Bedchamber, sent detailed bulletins during the days leading to the death of Queen Caroline on November 20, 1737. Some letters are more personal as those of the artist Henry Robert Morland, who cleaned some of Hanbury-Williams's paintings and negotiated to purchase them.

The Grand Tour features in the subseries: there are more than thirty letters from Thomas Steavens with lengthy reports from Vienna, Venice, Berlin, and other cities, during his multi-year travels. Two folders of letters from the poet Walter Harte cover his years of employment as tutor to the son of Philip Dormer Stanhope, Lord Chesterfield, on his grand tour; also present are letters from his student Stanhope. Hanbury-Williams offered support to other young men studying abroad, including Edward Craggs Eliot (also tutored by Harte), Richard Sutton in Gottingen, and George Yonge in Leipzig.
Hanbury-Williams's social intimates are well represented by two folders of letters each from Henry Harris and Thomas Winnington, and eight folders from Henry Fox, Baron Holland.

The writers in this subseries predominately were British citizens writing from the United Kingdom, but some wrote while traveling abroad. While the content of their letters—such as those of Richard Combe—may appear quasi-diplomatic due to foreign contacts and reports of events mentioned, the men were not known to be operating as agents of any government.

did.unittitle: Welsh and Herefordshire Friends and Colleagues

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This subseries contains letters from constitutents and friends in Herefordshire and Monmouthshire—Leominster in particular—as well as a few political documents related to Hanbury-Williams's parliamentary career. He had won the seat for Monmouthshire long held by his father when his father died in 1734, was reelected in 1741, and held it until his brother Capel won it in 1747. Also in 1741, through the influence of his wife's family, he was appointed lord lieutenant of Herefordshire, and in 1744 elected the high steward of the corporation of Leominster for life. In addition, in October 1744, Hanbury-Williams was installed as a Knight of the Bath.

Unlike correspondence in other parts of the collection, many letters here were addressed to Hanbury-Williams as "Knight of the Bath," "Member of Parliament," or both, and sent to him at Coldbrook [or Coldbrooke], Whitehall, or at "his Lodgings at Bath." Writers from Bath, Monmouth, Leominster, and the region ask for political or financial assistance, and favors such as various military and other appointments for friends and family. In many instances Lord John Bateman (1721-1802) of Shobdon Court in Leominster is mentioned; he assumed the post of Lord Lieutenant of Herefordshire in 1747. Also referenced in letters is a visit Hanbury-Williams made to Ruperra Castle, a Morgan family property in Caerphilly, Wales.

One of the major correspondents here is Hanbury-Williams's close friend Rev. James Birt, whose more than forty letters cover local news of friends, the Hanbury family, local and federal politics, and literature. The collection also has thirteen of Birt's replies, which are filed after his letters to Hanbury-Williams. Other notable (for volume) correspondents are William Fortune, Rev. Benjamin Lewis, and Joseph Pember; also in that group is Thomas Williams, the property manager at Coldbrook.
At the end of the subseries is a poll book from Leominster recording the votes in 1747, and a set of reports on actions taken in Parliament between 1747 and 1752, with three political documents.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Correspondence and Documents.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Writing for the Union Society in Bristol.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Reverend James Birt, a close friend of Hanbury-Williams, was lecturer at the chapel at Bream, a village in the Forest of Dean, West Gloucestershire, England, from 1743 until his death in 1801. The letters present from Hanbury-Williams to Birt are likely those mentioned by Birt in a 1796 letter to Hanbury-Williams's grandson George Capel-Coningsby, Lord Viscount Malden and later Earl of Essex (see Box 47, folder 13), who had intended to write a biography of Hanbury-Williams.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letter of July 5, 1748 has postscript by Richard Evans, a secretary to Hanbury-Williams.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: A family friend from nearby Llanfoist with a decades-long army career, Chambre was called to manage properties after Hanbury-Williams's death. His daughter Margaret (1733-1763) married Hanbury-Williams's brother George in 1748.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
