scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Williams was a tenant farmer residing near Coldbrook. The folder includes a letter from C. Tomkins, who wished Williams to appeal to Hanbury-Williams on his behalf, and the last letter in the folder is one that Williams wrote to Lord Essex regarding a meeting of Hanbury-Williams's Monmouthshire tenants.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Hanbury-Williams wrote from Dresden to an unidentified clergyman in Wales giving his first impressions of the place.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "An Exact Copy of the Poll taken at the General Election of Member to serve in Parliament for the said Borough on Tuesday the Thirtieth day of June 1747 Before Michael Stephens High Bailiff." This is a small manuscript in a stiff-board binding covered in green paper, with a printed CH-W number label. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 91; the Phillipps number is 11399.

did.unittitle: Reports of House of Lords and House of Commons Actions

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Precis of actions taken in Parliament sent to Hanbury-Williams in Dresden; manuscript in an unidentified hand.

did.unittitle: List of offices of the Exchequer, Treasury, and Ordnance prepared by Thomas Barker
Petition to supply grain for the poor and indigent
Brief for Haverford West petition regarding voting

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subseries holds letters from merchants with whom Hanbury-Williams had a relationship for purchasing or shipping goods unrelated to his appointments overseas; records for merchandise and shipping related to his diplomatic career are filed in Series I, Box 21.

Many of the merchants filed here dealt in cider and wine, Bridget Allan in particular, but also represented are goldsmiths (John Dowson and Simon LeSage), a butcher (Jane Mathews), a tailor (W. Davids), and the sculptor Thomas Carter (1702-1756), who had been commissioned to create a chimneypiece. London lace and textiles merchant Catherine Giberne sent a bill for lace, fans, and the twenty-four pounds he still owed her from Fanny Hanbury-Williams's 1754 wedding to Lord Essex. London booksellers are represented by Robert Dodsley and Thomas Osborne. Also present here are a dozen letters from two of Hanbury-Williams's cousins, Capel Hanbury and Osgood Hanbury (son of John Hanbury of Tower Street), who worked alone and together as freight forwarders.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically by vendor.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: In late November 1737 Hanbury-Williams was appointed paymaster of Marines. In 1745 a House of Commons committee began an investigation of the unit's procedures, and though Hanbury-Williams was never implicated, the months-long inquiry distressed him greatly. He resigned the position in December 1746.
See also a letter from Herbert filed with Hanbury-Williams's estate papers, Box 47, folder 25.

did.unittitle: Account of the fitting out and sailing of squadrons

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A report titled "An Account of all the Orders that have been given by the Commissioners for executing the office of Lord High Admiral of Great Britain since the 1st day of February 1737 relating to the fitting out and sailing of any of His Majesty's Squadrons or Ships of War with the Dates of such Orders, the number and rates of Ships, the places to which the same were thereby directed to sail, and the time of their respective sailing. Prepared in pursuance of an humble Address to His Majesty from the Honorable House of Commons of the 26th November last" [1736].
The report is in a stiff-board binding covered in blue paper, with printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 44; the Phillipps number is 11380.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Herbert had a career as a government clerk and secretary before being appointed by Hanbury-Williams as assistant paymaster of Marines on July 14, 1740. He died in 1769.

did.unittitle: Report of the Committee for inquiring into the State of the Marines

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The report, issued by Sir William Yonge to Henry Pelham, paymaster general of His Majesty's Guards, Garrisons and Land Forces, records the interviews of deputy paymasters Edmund Herbert and Edmund Jones, and captain Alexander Wilson. The second part is issued to Charles Hanbury-Williams, and is dated May 1, 1745.
The report is in a stiff-board binding covered in blue paper, with printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 45; the Phillipps number is 11381.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains a range of papers that stand separate from Charles Hanbury-Williams's diplomatic, government, and political careers: here they center on his family, his literary interests, and matters pertaining to his estate. The earliest item in the series is a poem from 1717 and the latest is a real estate accounting from his estate papers.

Foremost in the series are the letters Hanbury-Williams received from and sent to his wife Frances, his daughters Fanny and Charlotte, and his son-in-law Lord Essex. For all four, both sides of the correspondence are present, but the two sides were not interfiled; letters from each person to Hanbury-Williams precede folders of letters he sent them. Two-way correspondence is also here for two of his younger brothers, George and John; there are a greater number of incoming letters from his older brother Capel. Also of interest are folders of material filed with the letters from Elizabeth Mary Russell Capell, Lady Essex, regarding the negotiations for the marriage of her son William to Fanny Hanbury-Williams in 1754. Additional letters to and from most of the family members in this subseries also appear in Series IV, Capell, Coningsby, and Hanbury Family Papers; none of that material is addressed to or received from Charles Hanbury-Williams.

The subseries Personal Papers includes letters from his physicians John Oldfield and John Truesdale, and appeals from men regarding the death of Jeffrey French and the estates of Hanbury-Williams's maternal grandfather Richard Jones, Lord Ranelagh (1638-1712) of Ireland. Various financial and legal documents reveal some of Hanbury-Williams's other interests, such a list of seeds received from the American botanists John and William Bartram, a list of the Right Worshipful Company of Merchant Advisors of England, and two manuscript library catalogues. Thomas Philllipps suggested those may have been inventories of Lord Coningsby's book collection at Hampton Court Castle in Herefordshire, though there is no other evidence for that claim. A third list of book titles is tipped in at the front of a volume of letters and reports received from foreign envoys in 1752 (Hanbury-Williams volume 18, Phillipps volume 10889 (Box 11).

The subseries Literary Works holds writings by or attributed to Hanbury-Williams as well as works by others such as Lady Mary Wortley Montagu; that piece and some of the other writings may have formerly been owned by his father-in-law Lord Coningsby..

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series III is organized into four subseries: Correspondence with Family Members, Personal Papers, Literary Works, and Estate Papers.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The eldest daughter of Charles and Frances Hanbury-Williams, Fanny was born on March 4, 1735, and married William Capel, Lord Essex, in August 1754. She gave birth to three children, two of whom lived to their majority; she died on July 19, 1759, likely of diptheria. Fanny had a very close relationship with her father, who died three months later, not knowing that she had passed before him.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fanny Hanbury-Williams Essex's correspondence with her father spans sixteen years, and their letters comprise the largest correspondence group in the papers. Topics covered include innoculation, family activities, news, and travel, social events, and fashion; her letter of February 20, 1755, encloses a list of Dresden porcelain tableware. Letters to her father in the earlier years are often sent by both Fanny and Charlotte writing into the same letter, and letters to Fanny in those same years are addressed to both daughters. Additional letters from and about Fanny are in Box 43, folders 1-3, with material regarding her marriage negotiations.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Lady Essex was the wife of William Capell (1697-1743), third Earl Essex, and the mother of William Anne Holles Capel, Lord Essex. Along with Charles and Frances Hanbury-Williams, she negotiated the marriage of her son to Fanny Hanbury-Williams.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The folder holds legal and financial documents relevant to the engagement, along with correspondence from family members on both sides. Among the negotiation documents are statements of presumed/planned inheritances of Charles and Frances Hanbury-Williams which infer inheritances coming to Fanny Hanbury-Williams, as well as communications from attorney Francis Capper at Lincoln's Inn.

did.unittitle: Lady Essex's letters to Henry Fox, Baron Holland

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series of letters from Lady Essex to Henry Fox regarding engagement negotiations; some are marked that they were sent to Hanbury-Williams from Fox.

did.unittitle: Essex, William Anne Holles Capel, Earl of, 1732-1799

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Lord Essex became the son-in-law of Charles Hanbury-Williams after marrying his daughter Fanny in 1754.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: A step-sister of Lord Essex.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The mother of Charles Hanbury-Williams, she was the daughter of Sir Edward Ayscough of Kelsey, Lincolnshire, and the second wife of John Hanbury of Pontypool (1664-1734).

did.unittitle: Letters from Bridget to Charles Hanbury-Williams
Letters from Bridget to Frances Hanbury-Williams

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The older brother of Charles Hanbury-Williams, Capel was the third son and major inheritor of of their father John Hanbury of Pontypool.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The second eldest brother of Charles Hanbury-Williams, Edward died after a fall from his chaise.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The younger brother of Charles Hanbury-Williams who married Margaret Chambre (1733-1763) in 1748; they were a favorite uncle and aunt of the Hanbury-Williams daughters.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: A Hanbury cousin, he was a London merchant and known as John Hanbury of Tower St. He was the son of Charles (1677-1735) and Grace (-1710) Hanbury, and the father of merchant Osgood (1731-1784).

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The eldest Hanbury brother, John Hanbury married Anne Price, a neighbor in Llanfoist. He was to have inherited the family estates from his widowed mother, but died before she did.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: A nephew and the son of Capel Hanbury.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The youngest of the Hanbury brothers, Thomas Hanbury had a career in the Royal Navy.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Unidentified relation.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Frances Coningsby Hanbury-Williams was the wife of Charles Hanbury-Williams; they married in 1732.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A series of letters written by Frances Hanbury Williams to her husband regarding their separation and other family matters. The letters were formerly bound together; the Hanbury-Williams number was 39 and Phillipps number was 26018.

did.unittitle: Kildare, Elizabeth Fitzgerald, Countess of, 1664 or 1665-1758

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Elizabeth Jones Fitzgerald, the second wife of John Fitzgerald (1661-1707), 18th Earl of Kildare, was the maternal aunt of Lady Frances Coningsby Hanbury-Williams and her sister Margaret Coningsby Newton. The Fitzgeralds had no children, and the sisters stood to inherit their aunt's estate.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Countess Coningsby was the sister of Frances Hanbury-Williams; she married Michael Newton, fourth Baronet of Barrscourt and Culverthorpe.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The second of the two children born to Charles and Frances Hanbury-Williams, Charlottte married Robert Boyle-Walsingham in 1759; he was born in 1735 and was lost at sea 1780.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Charlotte's letters to her father date from 1751 to 1758. Earlier letters to him were co-written with her sister Fanny, and are filed with Fanny's letters in Box 42.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The 1759 letter here was written from Charlotte to James Price, who was attending to Hanbury-Williams's affairs at the end of his life.

did.unittitle: "Character of the Hon. Mrs. Walsingham, wrote by herself"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: While not a family member, Edward Young, of Eton College, wrote to Hanbury-Williams about the education of John ("Jack") Hanbury (1749-1819), the son of George Hanbury, as he was funding his tuition.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Correspondence and Documents.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A chronological list of all members of the company drawn up at Hamburg by Secretary John Manning. The tally begins in 1621 with Sir Robert Anstruther, ambassador to Denmark, and ends in 1758 with Hanbury-Williams.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript shelf list, 24 pages, bound in pulpboard covered in green paper with a blue cloth spine. The Phillipps catalogue cites it as "Catalogue of Books, apparently at Hampton Court." It has a printed CH-W number label on the spine and a printed Phillipps number label on the front cover. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 86; the Phillipps number is 11403.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript catalogue of 100 pages, listing titles alphabetically within formats: folios, quartos, octavos, and duodecimos. The lists are followed by two invoices (1748-1749) with titles from Georg Konrad Walther (-1778), a Dresden publisher, tipped in. Those pages are followed by two additional lists of titles of octavos and folios. The Phillipps catalogue lists the volume as: "Catalogue of (apparently) the Library at Hampton Court" and describes it as "Books bought by Sir C. H. Williams, from Walther, the Prussian Bookseller of Voltaire, with Walther's Autograph."

The manuscript is bound in pulpboard covered in green paper with a blue cloth spine. It has a printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 37; the Phillipps number is 11406.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Writings by Hanbury-William, and Writings by Others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The material in this subseries is entirely manuscript and largely poetry written out in a variety of hands, mostly unsigned but attributed to Hanbury-Williams. One volume of verses (Hanbury-Williams volume 65, Phillipps volume 23965) had been disbound in the 1990s. The loose poems were checked against the Folger Library's "Union First Line Index of English Verse," which encompasses verses in eight libraries in the US and UK, and three bibliographic collections (ESTC, STC, and Wing); verses with authors cited in collections were filed as "Writings by others."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An 82-page compliation of manuscript poems composed in a variety of hands on a variety of papers. The volume is bound in a stiff-board binding covered in dark blue paper with a blue cloth spine. It has printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 85; the Phillipps number is 11394.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph manuscript poems cover 94 leaves (the full volume), with poems arranged/dated chronologically from 1731 to 1743. Most of the material was published in his Works , and the album pages were annotated in pencil in the twentieth century with those citations.

The volume is in a stiff board binding covered with marbled paper with a calf spine. It has printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 69; the Phillipps number is 10930.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Autograph manuscript writings cover 57 leaves (about half of the volume) in an otherwise blank book, with poems arranged/dated chronologically from 1743 to 1744. Most of the material was published in his Works, and the album pages were annotated in pencil in the twentieth century with those citations.

The volume is in a stiff board binding covered with marbled paper with a calf spine. It has printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 70; the Phillipps number is 10930.

did.unittitle: "To Doctor Garnier and Mr Peirce - A Grateful Ode"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poem written "In return for the extraordinary humanity they both shewd to my Eldest Daughter, written at Coldbrook 1753."

did.unittitle: "Nature that loves new schemes to try"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

did.unittitle: "Verses by Sr. C. Hanbury Williams addressed to his daughter Lady Essex"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An untitled 59-page critique of another author's unidentified writing, seemingly on the topic of politics. Lines on page 5 outline the biography of (unnamed) Thomas Pelham-Holles, Duke of Newcastle, stating "The Noble Duke, who now presides at the Head of the Treasury," suggesting a date for the manuscript as 1754-1756. "Athelstan & Barbarossa" is inscribed inside the booklet's front cover, and the first page begins in the middle of a sentence, suggesting that it was preceded by another volume. Its title in the Phillipps catalogue is "Censure of a Literary Clergyman (a fragment)."
The manuscript is bound in marbled paper wrappers, with printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 88; the Phillipps number is 11396.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A manuscript of a play, unsigned and undated, attributed to Hanbury-Williams by Thomas Fitzroy Fenwick. Its title in the Phillipps catalogue is: "Zaida, a Theatrical Composition."
The manuscript is bound in tooled leather. It has printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 92; the Phillipps number is 10894.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A single-volume, 139-page manuscript biography of Frederick William I (1688-1740), King of Prussia and Elector of Brandenburg from 1713 through his death. The manuscript, unsigned, in French and in an unidentified hand, gives a chronological accounting of his life beginning in 1713.

The volume is bound in a stiff-board binding covered in light blue paper with a blue cloth spine; the text block has gilded edges. It has printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 37; the Phillipps number is 11377.

The manuscript is bound in a stiff paper wrapper covered in green paper with a blue cloth spine. It has the remnant of a printed Phillipps number label on the front cover. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 92; the Phillipps number is 11397.

arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Correspondence and Documents.

did.unittitle: Account of mortgage on Lanover Farm
Capell, Coningsby, and Hanbury Family Papers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series holds a collection third-party letters and documents that were received or sent by members of Charles Hanbury-Williams's family and his extended family through the 1754 marriage of his daughter Fanny to William Anne Holles Capel, Lord Essex. While he may be discussed in some of the letters, particularly those of Fanny to her uncle George Hanbury, no letters in the series were written to or by Charles Hanbury-Williams. This series also contains the earliest and latest items in the collection, both predating and postdating Hanbury-Williams's life; they are found in the papers of his father-in-law, Sir Thomas Coningsby and in the Hanbury Family Papers.

The first subseries, Capell and Essex Family Papers, holds correspondence files for Frances ("Fanny") Hanbury-Williams Capel and her husband William, Lord Essex, as well as his mother and sisters, who received letters from each other and friends. The subseries mostly holds single items, with the exception of the more than twenty letters from Albinia Townshend Brodrick, an Essex cousin-by-marriage who writes to sisters Anne and Diana Capell. Her lengthy letters cover many topics of London society, relationships, travel, and events. Also filed here is a single letter from Fanny Capel to her sister Charlotte Hanbury-Williams Walsingham. Note that Lord Essex's parents and sisters spelled their surname Capell.

The second subseries holds material related to/formerly owned by Thomas Coningsby (1656-1729), the father of Hanbury-Williams's wife Frances. Lord Coningsby's seat was Hampton Court Castle in Herefordshire, where he owned collections of books and historical records from the area that may be represented in one or both of the manuscript library inventories in Hanbury-Williams's Personal Papers in Series III (Box 45).

The third subseries is prominent in holding the correspondence of Hanbury-Williams's wife Frances, and his brothers, cousins, and nephews, in particular his brothers George and Capel Hanbury. George Hanbury's files contain two folders of letters to him from his niece Fanny Capel.

The letters in this series were in the mix of papers coming from the Hanbury-Williams properties in Wales and, as with the rest of the collection, had been bound into volumes with unrelated material; the volumes were taken apart in the 1990s. Biographical notes can also be found in the preceding series. Some of the correspondence here has to do with Charles Hanbury-Williams's estate; other related documents can be found at the end of Series III.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three subseries: Capell and Essex Family Papers, Thomas Coningsby Papers, and Hanbury Family Papers.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The older sister of William Anne Holles Capel, Lord Essex.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The daughter of Thomas Townshend (1701-1780) and Albinia Selwyn Townshend (1714-1739), she was the niece of George Augustus Selwyn. In 1752 she married George Brodrick, 3rd Viscount Midleton (1730-1765), who was the son of Alan Brodrick and Mary Capell (1700-1762), the aunt of Anne Capell and Lord Essex.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Capel-Coningsby was the eldest son and heir of William Anne Holles Capel, Earl of Essex (1732-1799), and his first wife Frances ("Fanny") Hanbury-Williams; he was styled Viscount Malden until 1799, and Earl of Essex after the death of his father.

did.unittitle: Report on the Glen-melleure Mine in County Wicklow

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Daughter of Charles Hanbury-Williams.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Thomas Coningsby (1656-1729), the father of Lady Frances Hanbury-Williams and father-in-law to Charles Hanbury-Williams, was a prominent Whig politician in the reigns of William and Mary, Queen Anne, and George I. Lord Coningsby entered the House of Commons as member for Leominster, Herefordshire, in 1679 and remained until the election of 1710. In 1698 he married as his second wife Lady Frances Jones (1674–1715), the youngest daughter of Richard Jones, Earl of Ranelagh, an Irish peer and paymaster-general of the forces.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Correspondence and Collections.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The correspondence filed here is both to and from Coningsby. It came from three formerly bound volumes, 40, 72, and 80, and was merged when the papers were processed in 2023. Many of the letters use numbers to represent people discussed within the text.

Volume 80 was untitled, and was in a stiff-board binding covered in dark blue paper with a blue cloth spine. It has printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Phillipps number is 11401.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letter from the Lord Archbishop of Dublin (1703-1729), written from Mount Merion in Dublin.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains letters from Marborough and drafts of Coningsby's letters to him.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Drafts of four letters by Coningsby to a recipient identified in docketing as "39."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters to Coningsby and drafts of letters by him; sender and recipients are unidentified.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This key was with Coningsby's letters in Hanbury-Williams volume 40.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material in this subseries was described by antiquary Thomas Fitzroy Fenwick (1856-1938), grandson of its former owner Sir Thomas Phillipps, as "Coningsby's collections," sometimes with no further description. It is a mix of bound manuscripts in a variety of hands from various time periods; much of the material is historical documentation but also includes poetry.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written in German Kurrent script by an unknown author, the diary covers thirty-one dated sessions of what appear to be government meetings. The 52-page document had been bound in Hanbury-Williams volume 80 (Phillipps 11401) with letters from and to the Duke of Marlborough.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The 56-page volume contains two sections of Coningsby's undated financial accounts (Phillipps's catalogue dates it as "circa 1714"), but it is mostly filled with copied literary works, including a translation of a letter from Pliny to Minutius Fundanus and poetry by John Hervey, Baron of Ickworth, and George Lyttelton. At some point a few blank pages were used by Thomas Hanbury (1722-1778), youngest brother of Charles Hanbury-Williams, as a school copy book for addition, penmanship, and Latin exercises.

The volume is in a stiff-board binding covered in calf with a blue cloth spine, with printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 84; the Phillipps number is 11402.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The volume consists of five separate documents bound together; they are unrelated in hand, format, date, and topic though four of the five concern Ireland. Further content description and analysis was supplied at the item level by David Brown, Department of History, Trinity College Dublin, in December 2023.

The volume is in a stiff-board binding covered in brown paper with a blue cloth spine, with printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 42; the Phillipps number is 11409.

did.unittitle: "A Project for service in Ulster 1595" by Ralph Lane

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Sir Ralph Lane (1532-1603) was an English explorer who was governor of the unsuccessful Roanoke Colony in Virginia (now Dare County, North Carolina) from 1585 to 1586; he later helped to colonize Ireland.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A seven-page tract and proposal addressed to Queen Elizabeth I, that outlines an early plan for the Plantation of Ulster. A transcription prepared by David Brown, Department of History, Trinity College Dublin, is available in the folder.
Another treatise with the same title by Sir Ralph Lane survives in The National Archives (UK) but it is ascribed a later date (1598): TNA SP 63/202/(4), no. 46.

did.unittitle: "An Acc of Monnyses payd for corne cattell, sheepe and such like, synce &c 24th of Marche 1625"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A one-page victualling list that includes prices for and suppliers of some military supplies.

did.unittitle: "A declaration of the presente Estate of the Englishe Paale of Irelande: And of Mr Nye the Charges with haue beene and have broughte the same to myserye, and Extreeme, Distresse &c&c and Exhibited to you, the Qeenes Ma. in Anno. Dm. 1600"

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: An 8-page report on the predatory behaviour of mercenary soldiers as they moved around the Irish Pale, many with their families and entourages, who lacked the means to pay for their own subsistence. The Pale was a Norman term, usually taken to mean a strip of land from Dublin to County Louth, along the East coast of Ireland.

did.unittitle: "The Prologue of Chaucer, reciting the Books he had written"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Title supplied by Sir Thomas Phillipps in his Catalogus librorum manuscriptorum in bibliotheca d. Thomæ Phillipps, Bart. A. D. 1837 .

Described in 2017 by Richard Beadle, Professor of Medieval English Literature and Palaeography, University of Cambridge as: "A post-medieval copy of Chaucer's 'Retraction (or Retractation)' which is found at the end of the Canterbury Tales in many, but not all of the extant early manuscripts of the work." (1 page)

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: The Irish Parliament under King Charles I was opened on July 14, 1634, at Dublin Castle, by Lord Deputy Thomas Wentworth.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The booklet contains speeches by Lord Deputy Thomas Wentworth (1593-1641) and Nathaniel Catelyn (1580-1637). It begins with a passage headed "Carolus Rex: Instructions to be obserued by our right trustie and welbeloved Cozin and Counceller Thomas Lord Vicount Wentworth Deputy Generall of our Realme of Ireland by our Councell there & by ye Deputy or other thiese Gouernor or Gouernors & Councell there which hereafter for the tyme shall be and by all other our officers and Mynisters whom it may severally & respectively Concerne." The passage sets out Wentworth's legislative programme for the 1634 parliament in 51 articles with the names of all 34 Privy Councillors listed at the end.

Thomas Wentworth's speech to the Irish Parliament at the opening of the session: "I shall crave leave with your good favour to take you to witnesse as of the greatest honour so of the greatest burthen I ever Sustained being instructed at this tyme in this high Courte to Communicate with you the Councell and the purposes of a wise and mighty kinge." The procedure is similar to that continued to this day by the King's Speech at the opening of the parliament of the United Kingdom. The purpose of the speech is to introduce the forgoing programme of legislation to parliament. (8 pages)
The Speaker's Speech 14th of July 1634 is a formal reply to Lord Deputy Wentworth's address delivered by the Speaker of the Irish House of Commons, Nathaniel Catlyn. (2 pages)

This is followed by a second, previously unknown speech by Catlyn, delivered on 25 November 1634, wishing Wentworth well with his undertaking and pointing out many of the difficulties he was likely to encounter trying to pacify the Irish and bringing them under the English legal system. The passage is written in a mixture of English and Latin and invokes the authority of King Charles I throughout. Starting on page 63, there is a list of the demands made by the speaker to the Lord Deputy concerning freedom of liberty and expression for members of parliament during the exercise of their duties. It is the only known copy of this address from the Speaker to the Lord Deputy. (14 pages)

did.unittitle: History of the Coningsby family, by Thomas Coningsby

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A 448-page manuscript (incomplete) in secretary hand on the history of the family and Herefordshire. Authorship attributed through a note inscribed in the margin of page 212 that mentions the author's grandmother Cecily Neville Coningsby (1604?-after 1652). Heneage Legge (1704-1759) inscribed his name inside the front cover, possibly indicating a provenance connection.

The volume is bound in tooled calf with a blue cloth spine and the royal coat of arms stamped in gold on the front and back covers. There are printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 33; the Phillipps number is 11405.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The 127-page manuscript covers the history of lands in Herefordshire around Marden. The volume is untitled but is inscribed inside the front cover "Query if this is not the Original MS of Lord Coningsby's History of Marden." "A Confirmation of Tintern Abby" begins on page 93.

The volume is in a stiff-board binding covered in brown paper with a blue cloth spine, with printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 41; the Phillipps number is 11408.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A 31-page manuscript in secretary hand titled inside, "Mr. Littletons Argument on the behalf of Mr. Stephens for not appearing at the Coronation of the Order of Knighthood." Edward Stephens, of Little Sodbury, Gloucestershire, was defended by Edward Littleton and Robert Mason in a hearing that took place in November 1630.

The volume is in a stiff-board binding covered in marbled paper with an octagonal paper label on the cover and printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 26; the Phillipps number is 10886. It was purchased from H. P. Kraus in 1981.

did.unittitle: Notes related to Robert Spencer, Earl of Sunderland, 1701-1729

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three pages of notes of engagements Coningsby was under with Lord Sutherland.

did.unittitle: "The true Account of John Harly's Pedigree & Rise …"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A 52-page manuscript in secretary hand containing excerpts from other documents that outline the life of the ancestor of Coningsby's peers Robert Harley, first Earl of Oxford and Earl Mortimer, and his son and heir Edward Harley.

The volume is in a pulp-board binding covered in gray paper with a blue cloth spine, with printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 43; the Phillipps number is 11410.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Small, 16-page manuscript in secretary hand; the text continues in Latin. Its title in the Phillipps catalogue is: "Jno. Joseph Grosma de Burgstein Liber de Vanitate Consiliorum."

The volume is in a pulp-board binding covered in green paper with a blue cloth spine, with printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine; there is also a printed CH-W label on the title page. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 87; the Phillipps number is 11395.

did.unittitle: "A Viewe of the Present State of Irelande discoursal by waye of a Dialogue between Eudoxus and Irenius"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A contemporary copy (82 leaves) of Edmund Spenser's manuscript.
The volume is in a contemporary vellum binding, with printed CH-W and Phillipps number labels on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 25; the Phillipps number is 11404.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized alphabetically.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Older brother of Charles Hanbury-Williams, he inherited the family estate at Pontypool.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Writing from Eton College about the progress made by his son John Hanbury (1744-1784).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: British lieutenant colonel writing from Wills Creek Camp (now Cumberland, Maryland) about the Battle of the Monongahela on July 9.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A letter to Hanbury about the recent death of George Bubb Dodington, Baron Melcombe (1691-1762) of Herefordshire, and a letter from him about property in Kensington.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Younger brother of Charles Hanbury-Williams.

did.unittitle: Essex, William Anne Holles Capel, Earl of, 1732-1799

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: John Hanbury was the oldest of the seven Hanbury brothers, and settled in Caerleon, Monmouthshire.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Osgood Hanbury was the son of a cousin, John Hanbury of Tower Street.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Writing from Eton College about student John Hanbury.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: John Hanbury of Pontypool was the father of Charles Hanbury-Williams.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Charles Williams was a native of Caerleon, and had a successful career in Smyrna when he fled Wales after killing his cousin in a duel. He returned to London as a financier in the 1690s and befriended John Hanbury. When he died in 1720 he left his estate to Hanbury, who entailed it for his son Charles, who then took the name Hanbury-Williams. For further information, see The Hanburys of Monmouthshire by Richard Hanbury Tenison (1995), chapter 4.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A volume of letters bound in gold-tooled and stamped green vellum, with a printed CH-W number label (and evidence of a Phillipps label, now missing) on the spine. The Hanbury-Williams volume number is 46; the Phillipps number is 10893.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Frances Coningsby Hanbury-Williams was the wife of Charles Hanbury-Williams.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters from her mother-in-law Bridget Hanbury are filed in Box 43, folder 10.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Margaret Coningsby was the first of two daughters born to Thomas Coningsby, Earl Coningsby (1656-1729), and his wife Frances Jones Coningsby (1672-1715). In 1730 she married Sir Michael Newton, 4th Baronet of Barrscourt and Culverthorpe. She was created Viscountess Coningsby of Hampton Court, Herefordshire, in 1717, and inherited the estate from her father; in 1761 the estate passed to her sister Frances Hanbury-Williams.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Williams was a tenant farmer residing near the family's home at Coldbrook, Wales. Later letters from him to Charles Hanbury-Williams are in Box 41, folder 9.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Listing of the assets inherited by Lady Frances Coningsby.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: John Hanbury-Williams was the son of George Hanbury (1715-1764), and a nephew of Charles Hanbury-Williams.

did.unittitle: Thomas Yeates letter and invoice for hats and epaulets shipped to Coldbrook

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Annotated to 1797.

did.unittitle: Title page from Madge's addresses to Christopher Twist-wit
Letter to an unidentified Hanbury brother

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series V is organized by Hanbury-Williams volume number.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series holds the boards and spines (most still attached) from forty-three volumes that formerly held Charles Hanbury-Williams's correspondence and other papers. At the box level, the bindings are listed by Hanbury-Williams volume number, followed by the (Phillipps) number. As the history of the binding of his papers is undetermined, the age of the binding material is also not known, but it is very likely some of the work was done during his lifetime.
See the Processing Information section of this collection guide for further information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This box holds bindings formerly on Hanbury-Williams volumes 9 (11379), 30 (11374), 31 (11375), and 32 (11376).
